variables.V_high: Logic HIGH voltage level (~5 V in TTL)
variables.V_low: Logic LOW voltage level (~0 V in TTL)

locked_facts: AND gate: Y = A·B — output is 1 only when ALL inputs are 1
OR gate: Y = A + B — output is 1 when ANY input is 1
NOT gate (inverter): Y = A̅ — output is the complement of input
NAND gate: Y = (A·B)̅ — output is 0 only when all inputs are 1; it is a universal gate
NOR gate: Y = (A+B)̅ — output is 1 only when all inputs are 0; it is also a universal gate
NAND and NOR are called universal gates because any Boolean function (AND, OR, NOT) can be constructed using only NAND gates or only NOR gates
De Morgan's theorems: (A·B)̅ = A̅ + B̅ and (A+B)̅ = A̅ · B̅ — these relate NAND/NOR to OR/AND with inverted inputs
truth_statements: A logic gate is a digital circuit that performs a basic Boolean operation on one or more binary inputs to produce a single binary output
NAND gate is the universal gate: NOT(A) = A NAND A; AND(A,B) = NOT(A NAND B); OR(A,B) = (A NAND A) NAND (B NAND B)
The truth table of a gate completely defines its behavior for all possible input combinations — 2 inputs give 4 rows, 3 inputs give 8 rows
De Morgan's first theorem states that the complement of a product equals the sum of complements: (A·B)̅ = A̅ + B̅
In practice, NAND gates are preferred for circuit design because they are easier and cheaper to fabricate and can implement any logic function

aha_moment.what_to_show: Three separate circuits built entirely from NAND gates: one implementing NOT, one implementing AND, one implementing OR. Input switches toggle and all three circuits produce correct outputs, proving NAND is universal. The equivalent standard gate symbol is shown next to each NAND construction.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Side-by-side: standard gate symbol vs its NAND-only equivalent, with matching truth table rows highlighting as inputs change
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see concretely that a single gate type (NAND) can replace all other gates — 'universal' is not just a label but a demonstrable fact

simulation_states.label: Basic gates and truth tables
simulation_states.description: Five gate symbols displayed: AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR. Each gate has its standard symbol, Boolean expression, and truth table shown beside it. Inputs A and B are at default values (0, 0). All outputs calculated and displayed. Color coding: HIGH = green, LOW = red.
simulation_states.emphasis: Learn the five basic gates, their symbols, and their truth tables

simulation_states.label: Gate operation with animated signals
simulation_states.description: A selected gate (AND by default) is enlarged with animated signal flow. Input signals travel as colored pulses along wires into the gate. The gate processes them and the output pulse emerges. The truth table on the right highlights the current row. All four input combinations cycle automatically with a pause between each.
simulation_states.emphasis: Watch signals flow through the gate and verify each truth table row visually

simulation_states.label: NAND as universal gate
simulation_states.description: Three circuits built entirely from NAND gates implementing NOT, AND, and OR. Beside each NAND construction, the equivalent standard gate symbol is shown. Input switches toggle and both the NAND construction and standard gate produce identical outputs. Truth tables for each match row by row. De Morgan's theorem is displayed: (A·B)̅ = A̅ + B̅.
simulation_states.emphasis: NAND can build any gate — this is why it is called the universal gate

simulation_states.label: Interactive logic lab
simulation_states.description: Student selects any gate type from a dropdown, toggles inputs A and B with switches, and sees the output in real time. The right panel shows the truth table with the current input combination highlighted. A circuit builder mode lets students chain gates: e.g., connect output of AND to input of NOT to verify it equals NAND. Boolean expression updates as gates are connected.
simulation_states.emphasis: Build and test logic circuits — verify truth tables and explore gate combinations

common_misconceptions.belief: NAND gate output is the same as AND gate output
trigger_phrases: no difference between NAND and AND
common_misconceptions.correction: NAND is the complement (NOT) of AND. When AND gives 1, NAND gives 0, and vice versa. NAND output is 0 only when all inputs are 1 — the exact opposite of AND which outputs 1 only when all inputs are 1.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show AND and NAND gates side by side with same inputs, highlighting that outputs are always opposite

common_misconceptions.belief: OR gate gives output 1 only when both inputs are 1, just like AND
trigger_phrases: OR mein bhi dono 1 hone chahiye
common_misconceptions.correction: OR gate gives output 1 when ANY input is 1 (or both). It gives 0 only when ALL inputs are 0. AND requires ALL inputs to be 1 for output 1. The key difference: OR = 'at least one', AND = 'all'.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Highlight the three rows where OR gives 1 (01, 10, 11) vs the single row where AND gives 1 (11) — use color contrast

common_misconceptions.belief: Universal gate means the gate that is used most commonly, not that it can replace other gates
trigger_phrases: sabse zyada use hone wala gate
common_misconceptions.correction: Universal gate means a gate that can be used to implement ANY other logic gate (AND, OR, NOT). NAND and NOR are universal because any Boolean function can be built using only NAND gates (or only NOR gates). The term refers to logical completeness, not popularity.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Demonstrate building NOT, AND, and OR using only NAND gates — proving the 'universal' property constructively
